summary: High-octane and well-drilled under Hajime Moriyasu (in charge since 2018) — the tactical opposite of the Netherlands. A settled 3-4-2-1 built on an aggressive high press and rapid counter-attacks. First side to qualify for 2026.
style: 3-4-2-1 — three centre-backs, two energetic ball-winners, and attack-minded wingers deployed as wing-backs to squeeze more dribblers in. Coordinated, intense high press; when it wins the ball high, five natural attackers pounce. Drops into a 5-4-1 low block under sustained pressure. Considerable freedom to rotate (even centre-backs overlap to become the most advanced player).

counterReliance.note: Aggressive high press + rapid, direct counter-attacks

strengths: Excellent, coordinated high press — 'well-drilled' (Tuchel, after Japan beat England 1-0)
Five natural attackers ready to break the instant they win it high
Flexible, rotational structure that drags opponents out of position
A counter-attacking ceiling proven by beating Spain 2-1 at the 2022 WC on just 18% possession
weaknesses: Can rarely exert control — reactive, quick-direct football cedes the ball
Shunting attackers to wing-back can stifle their creativity, especially in the 5-4-1 block

keyPlayers.name: Ayase Ueda
keyPlayers.note: Topped the Eredivisie scoring charts (25 in 27); movement geared to attacking crosses and the space behind (SkillCorner cross-receiver 80, runs-in-behind 65), and an effective presser

keyPlayers.name: Kaoru Mitoma
keyPlayers.note: The sweeping-counter finisher — scored the winner vs England off a fast break

tactics: Press aggressively, win it high, and break at speed with five attackers; sit in a 5-4-1 when pinned and spring the counter. Set pieces are a growing focus — long throws are in the plans and free-kick great Shunsuke Nakamura has joined the backroom staff.
notes: First nation to secure 2026 qualification, breezing through their group.
sources: The Athletic — 2026 World Cup tactical guide, Group F (5 Jun 2026)
John Walker (@johnwalker_1986) — pre-tournament tactical scouting, May–Jun 2026
